applicant_information.education.answer:
B.A. in Sociology, Fordham University, Bronx, NY, 1996

Continuing Education & Professional Development:
- Certificate in Nonprofit Management, Columbia University School of Professional Studies, New York, NY, 2012
- Project Management Professional (PMP), Project Management Institute, 2014 (active, renewed 2020)
- Certificate in Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion in the Workplace, University of South Florida (online), 2021
- Multiple workshops and trainings on trauma-informed care, motivational interviewing, and peer recovery support through local treatment centers and state alcohol and drug abuse services (2010–2023)

A.A.-Related Education and Training:
- Numerous local, regional, and national A.A. workshops on general service structure, Traditions, and Concepts (2010–present)
- Attended Regional Forums (Northeast) in 2015, 2018, and 2022
- Participated in area service workshops on the A.A. Service Manual, the Twelve Concepts for World Service, and group/conscience processes (2012–present)

experience_and_qualifications.current_an.answer:
I attended my first A.A. meeting on June 17, 2007, and I have maintained continuous sobriety since June 18, 2007. My home group is the "West Side Serenity" group in Manhattan, which meets on Tuesday and Thursday evenings at a community center on the Upper West Side. I have been an active member of this home group for over 10 years and currently serve as the group’s General Service Representative (GSR).

Current A.A. Experience:
- Home Group Member (West Side Serenity Group, New York, NY): I attend this group regularly (3–4 times per week) and participate in group conscience meetings, business meetings, and service activities. I sponsor several men, most of whom are in early recovery, and I stay actively involved in step work, traditions meetings, and service workshops organized by our district and area.
- General Service Representative (GSR), District 10, Area 49 (SENY): Elected in 2022, I represent my home group at district and area meetings. I regularly report back to my group on area and conference-related matters, including literature changes, service opportunities, and agenda items for the General Service Conference. I also help coordinate group participation in area assemblies and encourage members to get involved in service beyond the group level.
- District Corrections Committee Member: I currently serve on our district’s corrections committee, helping coordinate A.A. meetings at a local detention center in Manhattan. I participate in a rotation of volunteers who bring A.A. meetings into the facility twice per month. My responsibilities include coordinating literature, maintaining contact with facility staff, and ensuring adherence to both A.A. Traditions and institutional guidelines.

Past A.A. Service:
- Group Secretary (2010–2012): At my original home group in Brooklyn ("Sunrise Beginners Group"), I served as group secretary for two years. I was responsible for opening and closing the meeting space, maintaining the meeting format, organizing chairpersons, and ensuring that 7th Tradition funds were properly counted and distributed according to group conscience.
- Group Treasurer (2012–2014): After moving to Manhattan and joining my current home group, I served as treasurer for a two-year rotation. I set up a transparent, simple accounting system, provided monthly financial reports to the group, and oversaw regular contributions to the district, area, and G.S.O., following the group’s conscience and A.A. guidelines.
- Intergroup Representative (2014–2016): I represented my group at the local intergroup association. I participated in monthly meetings, shared group announcements, coordinated volunteers for the local hotline, and helped organize a city-wide A.A. anniversary event. I also supported outreach efforts to hospitals and treatment centers by helping maintain the meeting list and volunteering as a contact for newcomers.
- District Committee Member (DCM), District 10, Area 49 (2016–2018): I was elected DCM and served a full two-year term. In this role, I helped coordinate several district workshops on topics such as the Twelve Concepts, safety in A.A., and singleness of purpose. I worked closely with GSRs from multiple groups, encouraging participation in area assemblies and fostering a spirit of unity and inclusivity across the district.
- Area 49 Committee Service: I served on the Area 49 Literature Committee (2018–2020), reviewing conference literature proposals, gathering group feedback, and assisting with literature displays at area events. I also participated in a temporary ad hoc committee focused on improving hybrid meeting access across the area during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Conference and Regional Participation:
- Delegate’s Sharing Sessions: While I have not served as a delegate, I have actively participated in pre-conference and post-conference assemblies, providing informed group conscience on agenda items related to literature, accessibility, and technology. I have attended several Northeast Regional Forums and have engaged in sharing sessions with trustees, staff members, and other trusted servants.

Sponsorship and Service to the Fellowship:
- Sponsorship: I have been sponsoring other alcoholics since around my third year of sobriety. I typically sponsor 3–5 men at a time, meeting weekly or bi-weekly, working through the Twelve Steps, and encouraging participation in service. Many of the men I sponsor have become active in general service and corrections work. I emphasize the importance of the Traditions and Concepts, particularly in how they relate to service beyond the group level.
- Workshop and Panel Participation: I have chaired and participated in numerous panels and workshops at the district and area level on topics such as the Traditions, Concepts, service sponsorship, and the role of G.S.R.s. I have also shared my experience on panels addressing anonymity in the digital age, A.A. and technology, and carrying the message into correctional and treatment facilities.

Throughout my A.A. journey, I have tried to approach service with humility, a willingness to listen, and a commitment to group conscience. My experience at the group, district, and area levels has given me a practical understanding of the A.A. Service Manual, the Twelve Traditions, and the Twelve Concepts for World Service, and has reinforced my belief that our primary purpose—to carry the message to the alcoholic who still suffers—must remain at the center of all our actions.

experience_and_qualifications.occupation.answer:
I have over 25 years of professional experience, primarily in nonprofit management, program development, and project leadership, with a significant focus on behavioral health, community services, and organizational effectiveness.

Current Position:
- Director of Program Operations, New Horizons Community Recovery Center, New York, NY (2016–present)
  In my current role, I oversee day-to-day operations for a medium-sized nonprofit organization that provides outpatient recovery support, peer-led groups, and community-based services for individuals with substance use disorders and co-occurring mental health conditions. I manage a team of approximately 35 staff members, including program managers, clinicians, peer specialists, and administrative personnel.

  Key responsibilities:
  - Strategic Planning and Implementation: Collaborate with the Executive Director and Board to develop and implement multi-year strategic plans, annual program goals, and key performance indicators.
  - Budget and Financial Oversight: Help develop and manage an annual operating budget of approximately $4.5 million, ensuring programs remain financially sustainable and aligned with funder requirements. Work closely with the finance team to monitor expenditures and adjust programs as needed.
  - Program Development and Evaluation: Lead the design, implementation, and evaluation of new and existing programs, including recovery coaching, family support groups, and reentry services for individuals leaving incarceration. Use data collection tools and outcome measures to assess effectiveness and report to funders.
  - Staff Supervision and Development: Directly supervise five program managers and indirectly oversee all program staff. Conduct performance reviews, provide coaching and mentoring, and organize ongoing training on topics such as trauma-informed care, ethics, and cultural humility.
  - Stakeholder and Community Relations: Maintain relationships with partner agencies, government funders, hospitals, and community organizations. Represent the organization at city and state-level behavioral health coalitions and task forces.

Previous Positions:
- Program Manager, Urban Pathways Behavioral Health Services, Brooklyn, NY (2010–2016)
  Managed a portfolio of outpatient and community-based programs, including a drop-in center, intensive outpatient groups, and vocational support services for individuals in recovery.
  - Coordinated multidisciplinary teams (social workers, counselors, case managers) to deliver integrated services.
  - Developed and implemented program policies, procedures, and documentation standards.
  - Led quality improvement initiatives that reduced client no-show rates and improved retention in services.
  - Worked closely with clinical directors to ensure compliance with state regulations and accreditation standards.

- Senior Case Manager, Hope House Transitional Living, New York, NY (2005–2010)
  Provided case management services in a transitional housing program for individuals exiting residential treatment and correctional facilities.
  - Assisted clients with housing, employment, education, and linkage to outpatient treatment and mutual-help groups.
  - Facilitated life-skills workshops, including budgeting, relapse prevention, and communication skills.
  - Developed a resource guide for clients that included information on local A.A. and other mutual-help meetings.

- Earlier Career (Pre-Sobriety and Early Sobriety):
  Before entering the behavioral health and nonprofit sectors, I worked in a variety of administrative and customer service roles, including administrative assistant positions at a small law firm and a property management company. These jobs gave me experience with basic office operations, client communication, and time management. My drinking during that period limited my effectiveness, and I ultimately left those positions before getting sober in 2007.

Relevant Skills and Professional Strengths:
- Organizational Leadership: Extensive experience managing multi-program operations, supporting staff development, and implementing strategic initiatives in alignment with organizational missions and values.
- Project Management: Certified Project Management Professional (PMP) with a track record of launching new programs, implementing new data systems, and coordinating multi-stakeholder projects on time and within budget.
- Financial and Contract Management: Familiarity with grant management, government contracts, and nonprofit budgeting, including preparing reports for city and state agencies and private foundations.
- Policy and Governance: Regularly work with nonprofit boards and advisory committees, helping them understand operational realities, interpret data, and make informed decisions. Comfortable reading and applying bylaws, policies, and governance documents.
- Communication and Facilitation: Experienced in facilitating meetings, leading cross-functional teams, and presenting to boards, funders, and community groups. Skilled at translating complex information into accessible language for diverse audiences.

My occupational background has given me practical experience with board relations, governance structures, and the unique challenges faced by organizations that must balance mission, financial sustainability, and accountability to stakeholders. This experience is directly relevant to service on the A.A. World Services Board, where thoughtful oversight, collaboration, and adherence to spiritual principles are essential.

experience_and_qualifications.additional.answer:
Skills and Abilities:
- Nonprofit Governance and Board Relations: Over the last decade, I have regularly attended board meetings in my professional role, presented program reports, and worked closely with board committees (e.g., Program, Finance, and Governance). I understand the distinction between governance and management, and the importance of maintaining appropriate boundaries while still providing boards with the information they need to make informed decisions.

- Strategic Planning and Organizational Change: I have led or co-led several strategic planning processes involving staff, clients, and board members. This work has taught me how to facilitate group decision-making, balance multiple perspectives, and keep the mission at the center. I have experience guiding organizations through change, including implementing new technologies, restructuring programs, and adapting to funding shifts.

- Data-Informed Decision-Making: I am comfortable using data to inform decisions without losing sight of the human and spiritual aspects of our work. I have experience creating dashboards, tracking key performance indicators, and presenting data in a way that helps stakeholders understand trends and implications. I believe this skill can help the A.A. World Services Board evaluate projects and initiatives while remaining faithful to our primary purpose and spiritual principles.

- Communication, Writing, and Editing: I regularly draft grant proposals, program reports, policies, and training materials. I have a strong command of written English and an eye for clarity and consistency. I believe these skills could be useful in reviewing service materials, participating in committee work, and contributing to clear communication between the board, the Fellowship, and the General Service Office.

- Facilitation and Conflict Resolution: In both my professional and A.A. service roles, I have frequently facilitated meetings where strong emotions, differing opinions, and limited time were factors. I have experience using structured agendas, ground rules, and spiritual principles to help groups move toward an informed group conscience. I am comfortable listening to minority viewpoints and encouraging constructive discussion.

- Technology and Remote Collaboration: I have been involved in implementing hybrid and virtual meeting formats in both my workplace and my A.A. district. I am familiar with common videoconferencing platforms, collaborative tools, and basic cybersecurity considerations. I understand both the opportunities and challenges that technology presents for A.A., particularly regarding accessibility, anonymity, and inclusivity.

Personal and Life Experiences:
- Long-Term Sobriety with Ongoing Involvement: Having been sober since 2007, I have experienced A.A. through different phases of my life—early recovery, rebuilding my career, forming healthy relationships, and navigating loss and change without alcohol. I have remained actively involved in A.A. throughout, including during challenging periods such as the COVID-19 pandemic, when meetings moved online and many members struggled with isolation.

- Family Background and Diversity: I was raised in a working-class Puerto Rican family in the Bronx. Spanish was spoken in my grandparents’ home, and I grew up navigating multiple cultural contexts. This background has given me an appreciation for the importance of making A.A. accessible and welcoming to people of diverse backgrounds, including those who may feel marginalized or unsure if they belong.

- Lived Experience with the Criminal Justice and Treatment Systems: Prior to getting sober, I had several encounters with the legal system related to alcohol, including a DWI and a short period of probation. I also attended mandated outpatient treatment, where I was first introduced to A.A. Although I did not get sober immediately, these experiences gave me insight into the challenges faced by people who come into A.A. through courts, treatment, or other institutions. My later work in corrections-related A.A. service and reentry programs builds on this lived experience.

- Bilingual and Cross-Cultural Sensitivity: While English is my primary language, I speak conversational Spanish and have participated in some bilingual A.A. meetings. I am sensitive to the needs of members who may feel more comfortable sharing in their first language and to the importance of making A.A. literature and services accessible across language and cultural barriers.

- Experience with Illness, Loss, and Caregiving in Sobriety: During my sobriety, I have cared for an ailing parent and experienced the loss of close family members. I have walked through these experiences with the support of A.A., my sponsor, and my home group, learning firsthand how our program and fellowship can sustain us through grief and stress. This has deepened my appreciation for the spiritual foundations of our Traditions and Concepts.

Approach and Personal Qualities:
- Humility and Willingness to Learn: I do not view myself as an expert on A.A., but rather as a member who has been given opportunities to serve. I am aware that service at the world level requires a willingness to listen carefully, to seek guidance from group conscience, and to place principles before personalities.

- Respect for the Traditions and Concepts: My service experience has convinced me that A.A.’s Traditions and Concepts are essential to our unity and survival. I take them seriously and strive to apply them in practical ways, especially in matters of money, property, and authority.

- Ability to Work Collaboratively: I have experience working on multidisciplinary teams and in environments where collaboration is essential. I am comfortable working with individuals who have different viewpoints, communication styles, and backgrounds, and I strive to treat others with respect and patience.

I believe that this combination of professional skills, life experiences, and A.A. service can be of value to the A.A. World Services Board, particularly in areas related to governance, strategic planning, communications, and ensuring that our services remain accessible and responsive to the needs of the Fellowship worldwide.

experience_and_qualifications.current_or.answer:
I have not had any paid contractual relationships with A.A. World Services, Inc., A.A. Grapevine, Inc., or the A.A. General Service Board, Inc.

However, I have interacted with these entities in the following non-contractual ways:
- As a G.S.R. and former D.C.M., I have communicated with the General Service Office (G.S.O.) to request literature, clarify service-related questions, and coordinate information about area and district events.
- I have ordered A.A. literature and materials through standard channels (online and via local intergroup) for my home group and for correctional facility meetings. These transactions were handled in the same manner as any other group or member orders and did not involve any special contractual arrangements.
- I have submitted a few sharing letters and suggestions to G.S.O. related to service topics and literature, but none of these resulted in any formal relationship or compensation.

To the best of my knowledge and understanding, I do not have, nor have I ever had, any financial, contractual, or employment relationship with A.A. World Services, Inc., A.A. Grapevine, Inc., or the A.A. General Service Board, Inc. beyond ordinary literature purchases and routine communication as a member and trusted servant. If selected to serve, I am fully willing to disclose any relevant past interactions in more detail and to adhere to any conflict-of-interest policies that may apply.
